Jurist demos

Small typed languages for mathematical things

Little languages for dynamical systems, symbolic expressions, optimization models, Petri nets, typed graphs — with the Julia scientific ecosystem as their production semantics. Each is a handful of PureScript types: cheap to mint, one per domain, and a description that typechecks is a well-formed object of that domain.

Like any well-made eDSL, each admits more than one interpreter — and they are not equals. The reference interpreter is plain PureScript, so it runs wherever PureScript runs: prototype on Node or the BEAM and check the description does what you mean. Julia runs that same reference interpreter too — byte-identically, in the receipts just below — but it also has interpreters no other runtime can offer: analytic Jacobians and stiff/DAE solvers, rigorously proven roots and optima, dynamics derived functorially from category theory. Portability is the development story; the Julia-only interpreters are the reason to deploy.

lorenz · pendulum · differentiation · roots · optimization · petri · patterns · schema · migration

One description, three runtimes §

The Lorenz system, described once as a row-typed value — a misspelt field is a compile error. The same description runs through the same pure RK4 (integratePure, no FFI) on Node, the BEAM, and Julia — and the orbits agree byte for byte. Develop anywhere; deploy to the runtime with the native solvers.

The PureScript you write

lorenz
  :: SystemSpec
       ( x :: NumExpr, y :: NumExpr, z :: NumExpr )
       ( sigma :: NumExpr, rho :: NumExpr, beta :: NumExpr )
lorenz = system \s p ->
  { x: p.sigma * (s.y - s.x)
  , y: s.x * (p.rho - s.z) - s.y
  , z: s.x * s.y - p.beta * s.z
  }

-- the pure denotation: plain PureScript, no FFI —
-- it compiles on ANY backend
orbit = integratePure lorenz
  { x: 1.0, y: 1.0, z: 1.0 }
  { sigma: 10.0, rho: 28.0, beta: 8.0 / 3.0 }
  0.01
  5000

The lambda's s.x is var "x" — it builds an AST, not a closure. One description, two denotations (ADR-0007): this pure RK4, and Julia's native compiled vector field.

What three runtimes compute

Node maxZ = 47.8339540885982
BEAM maxZ = 47.8339540885982
Julia maxZ = 47.8339540885982
Julia byte-identical to the native RK4 over 5000 steps: true
max abs diff, first 200 steps: 0.0
The orbit every runtime agrees on (x–z projection)

Lorenz is chaotic — any difference in the arithmetic would diverge visibly within a few hundred steps. Bit-identity across three virtual machines is the strongest possible receipt that they ran the same description.

Data.SystemSpec (backend-agnostic) → Node · BEAM (purerl) · Julia (purejl) · data plotted here computed by integratePure on Node · source →

What the Julia interpreter buys you

The same typed value, given its most powerful meaning: solvers and proofs structurally out of reach for JS, WASM, or scipy.

A double pendulum, as the DAE it actually is §

In Cartesian coordinates the rod tensions are algebraic variables — no time derivative, closed by rigidity constraints. That makes this a differential-algebraic system, which scipy.solve_ivp and every JS ODE library structurally cannot solve. ModelingToolkit derives the index-1 form symbolically; an implicit stiff solver holds the rods rigid to ~10⁻⁸ through fully chaotic motion.

The PureScript you write

-- differential state · algebraic vars · parameters,
-- all row-typed
type DPState =
  ( x1 :: NumExpr, y1 :: NumExpr, x2 :: NumExpr, y2 :: NumExpr
  , u1 :: NumExpr, v1 :: NumExpr, u2 :: NumExpr, v2 :: NumExpr )
type DPAlg = ( t1 :: NumExpr, t2 :: NumExpr )  -- rod tensions

doublePendulum :: DAESpec DPState DPAlg DPParams
doublePendulum =
  daeSystem
    -- the differential part
    ( \s a p ->
        let ac = accels s a p
        in
          { x1: s.u1, y1: s.v1, x2: s.u2, y2: s.v2
          , u1: ac.ax1, v1: ac.ay1, u2: ac.ax2, v2: ac.ay2
          }
    )
    -- one rigidity constraint per tension
    -- (|p|² = L², differentiated twice)
    ( \s a p ->
        let ac = accels s a p
        in
          { t1: s.u1 * s.u1 + s.v1 * s.v1
              + s.x1 * ac.ax1 + s.y1 * ac.ay1
          , t2: (s.u2 - s.u1) * (s.u2 - s.u1)
              + (s.v2 - s.v1) * (s.v2 - s.v1)
              + (s.x2 - s.x1) * (ac.ax2 - ac.ax1)
              + (s.y2 - s.y1) * (ac.ay2 - ac.ay1)
          }
    )

accels gives the Cartesian accelerations in terms of the tensions, written once and shared by both lambdas. MTK solves the 2×2 tension system symbolically — never written in PureScript.

What Julia computes

1200 frames, 20 s of chaos — the browser only replays them
Max rod-length drift over the run: < 10⁻⁶ — the algebraic constraints held through fully chaotic motion (checked back in PureScript from sampled frames).

Data.DAESystem → Julia ModelingToolkit / OrdinaryDiffEq (implicit stiff solver) → SVG player · source →

Exact differentiation §

You describe an expression once, as a typed value. Julia differentiates it symbolically and hands the derivative back — the chain rule, fractions and all — typeset, never written by hand. Descriptions across, descriptions back.

The PureScript you write

-- one expression, described once, as a typed value
showcase :: NumExpr
showcase =
  num 10.0 * pow (var "x") (num 2.0)
    - var "x" * var "y"
    + sinE (var "y")
    + logE (var "x")

It crosses the seam once as a description — the shim never destructures the PureScript ADT (ADR-0002).

What Julia computes

f =

Data.NumExpr → Julia Symbolics / Latexify → KaTeX · full differentiation demo →

Rigorous roots — proofs, not guesses §

Each function is a typed NumExpr handed to Julia's IntervalRootFinding, which doesn't just find roots — it proves them: every root bracketed in a guaranteed enclosure, certified unique, with an exhaustive search so no root is missed — and "none" is itself a proof. A scipy/JS solver gives a number and a prayer.

The PureScript you write

let
  x = var "x"
  rootCases =
    [ { title: "x³ − 2x − 5", lo: -3.0, hi: 3.0
      , e: x * x * x - num 2.0 * x - num 5.0 }
    , { title: "sin x − x∕3  (three crossings)"
      , lo: -6.0, hi: 6.0
      , e: sinE x - divide x (num 3.0) }
    , { title: "x² + 1  — proven to have no real roots"
      , lo: -3.0, hi: 3.0
      , e: x * x + num 1.0 }
    , { title: "x⁴ − 10x² + 9  =  (x²−1)(x²−9)"
      , lo: -4.0, hi: 4.0
      , e: x * x * x * x - num 10.0 * (x * x) + num 9.0 }
    ]

-- for each case: proven enclosures, each certified
-- unique, none missed
rs <- provenRoots "x" c.lo c.hi c.e

Powers written as products so interval arithmetic stays valid over negative x. The expression crosses the seam once as a description; the proof comes back as data.

What Julia proves

Data.NumExpr → Julia IntervalArithmetic / IntervalRootFinding → KaTeX + SVG · source →

Provably optimal §

A 0/1 knapsack described once in PureScript. The obvious greedy heuristic (computed in pure PureScript) grabs the best value-per-weight item and stalls. Julia's JuMP/HiGHS mixed-integer solver doesn't guess — branch-and-bound proves the global optimum, closing the gap to zero. There is no MILP solver in the JS/WASM world to hand a model to.

The PureScript you write

let
  items =
    [ { name: "Telescope", w: 6.0, v: 12.0 }
    , { name: "Camera",    w: 5.0, v: 9.0 }
    , { name: "Tripod",    w: 5.0, v: 9.0 }
    , { name: "Drone",     w: 7.0, v: 8.0 }
    ]
  weights = map _.w items
  values  = map _.v items
  cap     = 10.0

  -- the myopic baseline, in pure PureScript
  greedy  = greedyValue weights values cap

-- the proof, on the Julia runtime
opt <- knapsack weights values cap

Greedy is the developer's mental model; knapsack builds a JuMP model with binary variables and lets HiGHS close the optimality gap.

What Julia proves

Data.Optimize → Julia JuMP / HiGHS · source →

Category theory, applied — AlgebraicJulia

Typed structures in, categorical machinery out: there is no applied-category-theory stack in the JS/WASM world. The toolkit a Minard could lean on.

Petri nets → functorial dynamics §

Two epidemic models as typed Petri nets — species and reactions, no differential equations anywhere in the description. Julia's AlgebraicPetri produces the mass-action dynamics by a functor from Petri nets to vector fields (not a hand translation); OrdinaryDiffEq integrates it. There is no applied-category-theory stack in the JS/WASM world to hand a description to.

The PureScript you write

sir :: PetriSpec
sir =
  { species: [ "S", "I", "R" ]
  , transitions:
      [ { name: "inf", inputs: [ "S", "I" ]
        , outputs: [ "I", "I" ], rate: 0.0003 }
      , { name: "rec", inputs: [ "I" ]
        , outputs: [ "R" ], rate: 0.1 }
      ]
  , initial: [ 990.0, 10.0, 0.0 ]
  }

-- SEIR adds a latent Exposed compartment;
-- the machinery is IDENTICAL
seir :: PetriSpec
seir =
  { species: [ "S", "E", "I", "R" ]
  , transitions:
      [ { name: "expose", inputs: [ "S", "I" ]
        , outputs: [ "E", "I" ], rate: 0.0004 }
      , { name: "onset", inputs: [ "E" ]
        , outputs: [ "I" ], rate: 0.2 }
      , { name: "recover", inputs: [ "I" ]
        , outputs: [ "R" ], rate: 0.1 }
      ]
  , initial: [ 990.0, 0.0, 10.0, 0.0 ]
  }

samples <- solve spec 0.0 200.0 200
laws    <- odeLaws spec   -- derived, typeset

The ODE laws on the right were derived functorially by AlgebraicPetri and typeset by Latexify — never written by hand.

What Julia derives & integrates

Data.Petri → Julia AlgebraicPetri / OrdinaryDiffEq → KaTeX + SVG · source →

Structural patterns, found by homomorphism search §

A module dependency graph and three structural motifs, described once in PureScript. Catlab finds every occurrence as the set of graph homomorphisms pattern → graph — subgraph matching generalized to any schema, the category doing the searching. The same engine finds circular dependencies, mutual dependencies, and diamonds — the machinery a Minard could use to flag architectural motifs in a code graph.

The PureScript you write

-- A → B means "module A depends on module B"
depGraph :: Graph
depGraph =
  { nodes: [ "Core", "Parser", "AST", "Eval"
           , "Pretty", "Codegen", "Cli", "Utils" ]
  , edges:
      [ edge 6 0, edge 6 1   -- Cli → Core, Parser
      , edge 1 2, edge 2 3   -- Parser → AST → Eval
      , edge 3 1              --   → Parser  (cycle!)
      , edge 4 5, edge 5 4   -- Pretty ⇄ Codegen
      , edge 0 7, edge 1 7   -- Core, Parser → Utils
      , edge 5 2              -- Codegen → AST
      ]
  }

patterns :: Array Motif
patterns =
  [ { name: "Circular dependency"
    , shape: "a → b → c → a", nodes: 3
    , edges: [ edge 0 1, edge 1 2, edge 2 0 ] }
  , { name: "Mutual dependency"
    , shape: "a ⇄ b", nodes: 2
    , edges: [ edge 0 1, edge 1 0 ] }
  , { name: "Diamond dependency"
    , shape: "a → (b, c) → d", nodes: 4
    , edges: [ edge 0 1, edge 0 2
             , edge 1 3, edge 2 3 ] }
  ]

ms <- matches depGraph p   -- Catlab searches

A match is a vertex map; PureScript canonicalises the rotations/orderings the search returns down to distinct occurrences.

What Catlab finds

Data.Pattern → Julia Catlab (homomorphisms, monic) → SVG · source →

Architectural motifs over a typed schema §

A typed code graph — modules, functions typed into modules, calls — as an instance of a Catlab ACSet schema. Each motif is a small instance of the same schema, and its type structure encodes the rule: two distinct pattern-modules match only cross-module structure. The typed-graph analogue of Hylograph's graphs, and the machinery a Minard could use to express architectural rules.

The PureScript you write

-- schema: Func —in_mod→ Module · Call —src,tgt→ Func
codeGraph :: CodeGraph
codeGraph =
  { modules: [ "Auth", "Db", "Api", "Util" ]
  , funcs:
      [ fn "login" 0, fn "hashPwd" 0    -- Auth
      , fn "query" 1, fn "connect" 1    -- Db
      , fn "handleReq" 2, fn "route" 2  -- Api
      , fn "log" 3, fn "validate" 3     -- Util
      ]
  , calls:
      [ edge 0 2, edge 0 1, edge 0 6
      , edge 2 3, edge 2 6
      , edge 4 0, edge 4 5, edge 5 2
      , edge 7 6, edge 4 7
      ]
  }

-- two distinct pattern-modules ⇒ only
-- cross-module structure can match
motifs :: Array Motif
motifs =
  [ { name: "Cross-module call"
    , shape: "f @A → g @B", nMod: 2
    , fnMod: [ 0, 1 ], calls: [ edge 0 1 ] }
  , { name: "Shared dependency"
    , shape: "f @A, g @B → h @C", nMod: 3
    , fnMod: [ 0, 1, 2 ]
    , calls: [ edge 0 2, edge 1 2 ] }
  ]

ms <- matches codeGraph mo   -- typed search
@present SchCode:  Func —in_mod→ Module · Call —src,tgt→ Func

What Catlab finds

Data.CodeGraph → Julia Catlab (typed @acset + homomorphisms) → SVG · source →

Functorial data migration §

A function-level call graph, migrated functorially to its module dependency graph — a Δ migration along the in_mod functor, where every call f → g becomes a module edge in_mod(f) → in_mod(g). Not a hand-written fold; a migration functor with categorical guarantees. The result is a plain graph — exactly the shape Hylograph lays out and renders.

The PureScript you write

codeGraph :: CodeGraph
codeGraph =
  { modules: [ "Auth", "Db", "Api", "Util" ]
  , funcs:
      [ fn "login" 0, fn "hashPwd" 0
      , fn "query" 1, fn "connect" 1
      , fn "handleReq" 2, fn "route" 2
      , fn "log" 3, fn "validate" 3
      ]
  , calls:
      [ edge 0 2, edge 0 3   -- Auth → Db   (×2)
      , edge 0 6, edge 1 6   -- Auth → Util (×2)
      , edge 2 6              -- Db → Util   (×1)
      , edge 4 0, edge 5 1   -- Api → Auth  (×2)
      , edge 5 2, edge 4 2
      , edge 4 3              -- Api → Db    (×3)
      , edge 4 6              -- Api → Util  (×1)
      , edge 0 1, edge 2 3   -- intra-module
      ]
  }

-- the migration: V ↦ Module, E ↦ Call,
-- src ↦ src⨟in_mod, tgt ↦ tgt⨟in_mod
raw <- moduleGraph codeGraph

-- PureScript aggregates the migrated multigraph
-- into weighted module dependencies
agg = aggregate raw

Catlab returns one module edge per call; the weights (×2, ×3) are the multiplicities — coupling strength for free.

What Catlab derives

Function call graph — the typed description
↓ Δ migrate along in_mod
Module dependency graph — derived functorially

Data.Migration → Julia Catlab (migrate, Δ along in_mod) → SVG · source →

The unglamorous part

Underneath is purejl, a from-scratch PureScript→Julia backend. It consumes CoreFn — the compiler's fully-elaborated core — so the whole language arrives by construction: typeclasses, row types, ADTs, the works (the row-typed SystemSpec machinery above is RowList-driven typeclass derivation, running on Julia). Core-library coverage: 50 built-in FFI shims (prelude, arrays incl. ST, strings, records, integers, numbers, …; Data.String.Regex is a deliberate loud-failure stub). Verified by differential testing against the JS backend as reference semantics: 422 of 426 outputs byte-identical, the other 4 documented representation choices (UTF-16 code units vs. codepoints).

The glamorous part is what it lets you mean.

Every left-hand pane above is the real eDSL source from its example; every right-hand pane is data computed by the Julia (or Node/BEAM) runtime and committed with the example. Each section also exists as a standalone, shareable page — the § links. ADR-0007.
